channel.title: Project Phasing and Certificate of Occupancy
channel.description: Finishing construction isn't finishing the project: certificates of occupancy, temporary COs, phased opening, and why phasing is a financial strategy.

item.title: Builder-Led Delivery of Multi-Family: A Primer
item.description:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

How a Multi-Family Project Actually Gets Built

A multi-family development is not one job but a chain of them - design, entitlement, financing, construction, lease-up - and how that chain is organized determines whether a project runs smoothly or fractures at every handoff. The organizing decision is the delivery model: who holds responsibility for turning an approved design into a finished, occupied building, and how the roles of designer, builder, and developer are structured around them. Builder-led delivery, where construction expertise leads the process from early design onward, has become a dominant approach precisely because it closes the gaps where traditional handoffs fail. Understanding how these roles and models fit together is the difference between a coordinated project and a series of expensive surprises, and the full treatment lives in Builder-Led Delivery of Multi-Family Developments. This primer maps the pieces.

The Three Roles in Every Project

Behind every apartment building are three distinct functions, sometimes held by three parties and sometimes combined. The developer originates the project - controls the land, arranges financing, carries the risk, and owns the outcome. The designer produces the drawings - architecture and engineering that must be both beautiful and buildable. The builder constructs it - the general contractor and trades who turn drawings into structure. Traditional delivery keeps these separate, with the developer contracting the designer and builder independently and refereeing between them. Integrated models combine roles, and the combinations are where the delivery-model choices live.

Why Construction Insight Belongs Early

The recurring failure of separated delivery is that design and construction knowledge arrive at different times. An architect designs, the drawings go out to bid, and only then does a builder - pricing the finished design cold - discover the details that are expensive or impractical to build. By then the design is fixed, the budget is set, and the fixes come as change orders and value engineering that erode both the design and the schedule. Builder-led delivery inverts this: construction expertise sits at the table during design, flagging cost and constructability while the drawings can still respond. On multi-family projects, where hundreds of repeated details multiply every decision across many units, that early insight compounds into real savings.

Delivery modelHow it's structuredWhere construction insight enters
Design-bid-buildDesign first, then bid to a builderLate - after design is complete
Construction-manager-at-riskBuilder advises during design, then builds under a priceEarly - as a paid advisor
Design-buildOne entity holds design and constructionContinuous - from day one
Developer-builderDeveloper and builder are the same firmTotal - the builder owns the outcome

Financing Sets the Rhythm

No amount of design or construction skill matters without money arriving in the right amounts at the right times, and multi-family projects run on a distinctive financing structure. Construction loans fund the build in stages, released against verified progress rather than all at once, and the whole project schedule beats to the rhythm of those draws. A builder who understands how lenders release money - and what documentation and inspection each draw requires - keeps the project liquid; one who does not can stall a job waiting on funds. Financing fluency is therefore part of delivery, not a separate finance-department concern.

Delivery Runs Past the Ribbon-Cutting

Finishing construction is not finishing the project. A multi-family building must pass inspections, earn its certificate of occupancy, and often open in phases so that early units generate income and lease-up while later ones complete. Sequencing that endgame - what must be done to occupy the first building or floor, how phasing aligns with the financing and the market - is as much a part of builder-led delivery as pouring the foundation. The models and roles that carry a project from concept through that final occupancy are exactly what a full treatment of builder-led delivery lays out.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is builder-led delivery?

An approach where construction expertise leads the development process from early design onward, rather than entering only after the design is finished. It closes the gaps where traditional design-then-bid handoffs fail, catching cost and constructability issues while the design can still change.

What are the three roles in a development project?

The developer originates and owns the project and carries the risk; the designer produces the architecture and engineering; the builder constructs it. Traditional delivery keeps them separate, while integrated models combine roles - and how they combine defines the delivery model.

Why does construction insight need to come early?

Because when a builder prices a finished design cold, the expensive or impractical details surface too late to change cheaply, arriving as change orders and value engineering. Early construction input flags those issues while the drawings can still respond, which matters enormously across a building's many repeated units.

What is the difference between design-build and developer-builder?

Design-build combines design and construction in one entity delivering to a developer-client. Developer-builder goes further - the developer and builder are the same firm, so the party building the project also owns its outcome, aligning every incentive under one roof.

Why does financing affect delivery?

Because construction loans release money in stages against verified progress, and the project schedule runs on the rhythm of those draws. A builder fluent in how and when lenders release funds keeps the project liquid; one who is not can stall the job waiting on money.

Does delivery end when construction finishes?

No - the project must pass inspections, earn its certificate of occupancy, and often open in phases so early units lease up while later ones complete. Sequencing that occupancy endgame is a core part of delivery, not an afterthought.

Which delivery model is best for multi-family?

It depends on the developer's capacity and goals, but integrated models that bring construction insight in early - construction-manager-at-risk, design-build, or developer-builder - tend to suit multi-family well, because the repetition across many units multiplies the value of catching issues before they are built dozens of times over.

Design-Build for Multi-Family Construction
item.content:encoded:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

How a Multi-Family Project Actually Gets Built

A multi-family development is not one job but a chain of them - design, entitlement, financing, construction, lease-up - and how that chain is organized determines whether a project runs smoothly or fractures at every handoff. The organizing decision is the delivery model: who holds responsibility for turning an approved design into a finished, occupied building, and how the roles of designer, builder, and developer are structured around them. Builder-led delivery, where construction expertise leads the process from early design onward, has become a dominant approach precisely because it closes the gaps where traditional handoffs fail. Understanding how these roles and models fit together is the difference between a coordinated project and a series of expensive surprises, and the full treatment lives in Builder-Led Delivery of Multi-Family Developments. This primer maps the pieces.

The Three Roles in Every Project

Behind every apartment building are three distinct functions, sometimes held by three parties and sometimes combined. The developer originates the project - controls the land, arranges financing, carries the risk, and owns the outcome. The designer produces the drawings - architecture and engineering that must be both beautiful and buildable. The builder constructs it - the general contractor and trades who turn drawings into structure. Traditional delivery keeps these separate, with the developer contracting the designer and builder independently and refereeing between them. Integrated models combine roles, and the combinations are where the delivery-model choices live.

Why Construction Insight Belongs Early

The recurring failure of separated delivery is that design and construction knowledge arrive at different times. An architect designs, the drawings go out to bid, and only then does a builder - pricing the finished design cold - discover the details that are expensive or impractical to build. By then the design is fixed, the budget is set, and the fixes come as change orders and value engineering that erode both the design and the schedule. Builder-led delivery inverts this: construction expertise sits at the table during design, flagging cost and constructability while the drawings can still respond. On multi-family projects, where hundreds of repeated details multiply every decision across many units, that early insight compounds into real savings.

Delivery modelHow it's structuredWhere construction insight enters
Design-bid-buildDesign first, then bid to a builderLate - after design is complete
Construction-manager-at-riskBuilder advises during design, then builds under a priceEarly - as a paid advisor
Design-buildOne entity holds design and constructionContinuous - from day one
Developer-builderDeveloper and builder are the same firmTotal - the builder owns the outcome

Financing Sets the Rhythm

No amount of design or construction skill matters without money arriving in the right amounts at the right times, and multi-family projects run on a distinctive financing structure. Construction loans fund the build in stages, released against verified progress rather than all at once, and the whole project schedule beats to the rhythm of those draws. A builder who understands how lenders release money - and what documentation and inspection each draw requires - keeps the project liquid; one who does not can stall a job waiting on funds. Financing fluency is therefore part of delivery, not a separate finance-department concern.

Delivery Runs Past the Ribbon-Cutting

Finishing construction is not finishing the project. A multi-family building must pass inspections, earn its certificate of occupancy, and often open in phases so that early units generate income and lease-up while later ones complete. Sequencing that endgame - what must be done to occupy the first building or floor, how phasing aligns with the financing and the market - is as much a part of builder-led delivery as pouring the foundation. The models and roles that carry a project from concept through that final occupancy are exactly what a full treatment of builder-led delivery lays out.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is builder-led delivery?

An approach where construction expertise leads the development process from early design onward, rather than entering only after the design is finished. It closes the gaps where traditional design-then-bid handoffs fail, catching cost and constructability issues while the design can still change.

What are the three roles in a development project?

The developer originates and owns the project and carries the risk; the designer produces the architecture and engineering; the builder constructs it. Traditional delivery keeps them separate, while integrated models combine roles - and how they combine defines the delivery model.

Why does construction insight need to come early?

Because when a builder prices a finished design cold, the expensive or impractical details surface too late to change cheaply, arriving as change orders and value engineering. Early construction input flags those issues while the drawings can still respond, which matters enormously across a building's many repeated units.

What is the difference between design-build and developer-builder?

Design-build combines design and construction in one entity delivering to a developer-client. Developer-builder goes further - the developer and builder are the same firm, so the party building the project also owns its outcome, aligning every incentive under one roof.

Why does financing affect delivery?

Because construction loans release money in stages against verified progress, and the project schedule runs on the rhythm of those draws. A builder fluent in how and when lenders release funds keeps the project liquid; one who is not can stall the job waiting on money.

Does delivery end when construction finishes?

No - the project must pass inspections, earn its certificate of occupancy, and often open in phases so early units lease up while later ones complete. Sequencing that occupancy endgame is a core part of delivery, not an afterthought.

Which delivery model is best for multi-family?

It depends on the developer's capacity and goals, but integrated models that bring construction insight in early - construction-manager-at-risk, design-build, or developer-builder - tend to suit multi-family well, because the repetition across many units multiplies the value of catching issues before they are built dozens of times over.

Design-Build for Multi-Family Construction

item.title: Design-Build for Multi-Family Construction
item.description:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

One Contract for a Building That Answers to Hundreds of People

Design-build is a delivery model in which a single entity holds responsibility for both designing and constructing a project under one contract. For a custom home it is a convenience; for a multi-family building it is a coordination strategy that scales. An apartment building multiplies every design decision across dozens of units, threads engineered fire, egress, and mechanical systems through a complex structure, and runs on a construction loan that punishes delay - conditions where the gap between a separate designer and builder becomes expensive. Design-build closes that gap by putting both under one roof, and understanding how it works illuminates why it has become so common in multi-family development.

How Design-Build Differs From the Traditional Path

The traditional model, design-bid-build, is sequential: the developer hires an architect, the completed design goes out to bid, a builder is selected, and construction begins under a separate contract. The two parties never share a contract with each other, so when a drawing meets a field reality, the developer mediates between a designer defending the drawings and a builder pricing the fix. Design-build collapses that structure. One entity - carrying or partnering with the architects, engineers, and construction team - is accountable for the whole result, and the internal handoffs that used to happen across contracts now happen within one organization, continuously.

Why Multi-Family Rewards the Model

Three features of apartment construction make design-build especially valuable:

Multi-family feature Why design-build helps
Repetition across units A detail refined once during design saves cost dozens of times over
Complex engineered systems Fire, egress, and mechanical coordination happens inside one team
Schedule-sensitive financing Overlapping design and construction compresses the timeline
Single accountability One entity answers for the whole outcome, not two pointing fingers

The repetition point is the sharpest. In a single-family house a poorly-detailed connection is one problem; in a hundred-unit building it is a hundred problems, and the money saved by catching it during design rather than in the field multiplies accordingly. Design-build's continuous cost and constructability feedback is what catches it.

Speed Through Overlap

Because one entity controls both phases, design and construction can overlap rather than running strictly in sequence - foundations can begin while upper-floor details are finalized, a practice sometimes called fast-tracking. On a project carrying a construction loan whose interest accrues daily, compressing the schedule this way translates directly into saved carrying cost. The traditional model, with its clean handoff from a finished design to a bidding process, cannot overlap the phases the same way, which is one reason schedule-driven multi-family projects lean toward integrated delivery.

The Trade-Off: Oversight for Integration

Design-build's efficiency comes at the cost of the independent check that separated delivery provides. When the designer and builder are the same entity, the developer loses the architect who, in the traditional model, watches the builder's work on the owner's behalf. This is not a fatal flaw - it is a reason to vet a design-build firm carefully, review its completed portfolio, and structure the contract with transparency into costs and decisions. Developers who choose design-build trade an independent overseer for a single accountable partner, and the trade favors them when the partner is proven. Evaluating that partner and structuring the relationship is part of the broader builder-led delivery decision.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is design-build?

A delivery model where one entity holds both the design and construction of a project under a single contract, rather than the developer hiring a separate architect and builder. It creates one point of accountability and lets design and construction inform each other continuously.

How is design-build different from design-bid-build?

Design-bid-build is sequential and split: the design is finished, then bid to a separate builder under a second contract. Design-build combines both roles in one entity, so the internal handoffs happen within one organization rather than across contracts the developer must referee.

Why is design-build common for apartment buildings?

Because multi-family projects multiply every design decision across many units, run complex engineered systems, and carry schedule-sensitive financing. Design-build's early, continuous construction input catches costly issues before they are built dozens of times, and its overlapping phases compress the schedule.

What is fast-tracking?

Overlapping design and construction so that early work - like foundations - begins before later design details are complete. It is possible under design-build because one entity controls both phases, and it compresses the timeline, saving carrying cost on the construction loan.

Does design-build reduce design quality?

Not inherently - design quality depends on the designers involved, and established design-build firms employ or partner with licensed architects and engineers. The key is reviewing the firm's completed work, since the model removes the independent architect who would otherwise check the builder.

What does the developer give up with design-build?

The independent oversight of a separate architect watching the builder on the owner's behalf. The developer trades that check for a single accountable partner, which is why careful vetting and a transparent contract matter more under design-build.

Is design-build always the right choice?

No - it suits developers who value speed, single accountability, and integrated budgeting, but a developer with a trusted independent architect and a fully documented design may prefer the traditional path or a construction-manager arrangement. The best model depends on the project and the team.

How does design-build affect the construction loan?

Favorably, in most cases - lenders value the schedule certainty and single point of accountability an integrated team provides, and the compressed timeline from overlapping design and construction reduces the interest that accrues before the building earns income. A design-build entity delivering a firm price early also gives the lender a clearer basis for sizing the loan, which can smooth the financing process compared with waiting for competitive bids on a finished design.

Can a developer bring their own architect into a design-build project?

Often yes, through a hybrid arrangement where the developer's architect handles concept design and the design-build entity develops the construction documents and builds. This preserves some independent design voice while keeping the constructability and cost advantages of integrated delivery. The responsibility boundaries between the two need to be defined clearly in writing so the single-accountability benefit is not lost.

Developer, Builder, and GC Roles Explained
item.content:encoded:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

One Contract for a Building That Answers to Hundreds of People

Design-build is a delivery model in which a single entity holds responsibility for both designing and constructing a project under one contract. For a custom home it is a convenience; for a multi-family building it is a coordination strategy that scales. An apartment building multiplies every design decision across dozens of units, threads engineered fire, egress, and mechanical systems through a complex structure, and runs on a construction loan that punishes delay - conditions where the gap between a separate designer and builder becomes expensive. Design-build closes that gap by putting both under one roof, and understanding how it works illuminates why it has become so common in multi-family development.

How Design-Build Differs From the Traditional Path

The traditional model, design-bid-build, is sequential: the developer hires an architect, the completed design goes out to bid, a builder is selected, and construction begins under a separate contract. The two parties never share a contract with each other, so when a drawing meets a field reality, the developer mediates between a designer defending the drawings and a builder pricing the fix. Design-build collapses that structure. One entity - carrying or partnering with the architects, engineers, and construction team - is accountable for the whole result, and the internal handoffs that used to happen across contracts now happen within one organization, continuously.

Why Multi-Family Rewards the Model

Three features of apartment construction make design-build especially valuable:

Multi-family feature Why design-build helps
Repetition across units A detail refined once during design saves cost dozens of times over
Complex engineered systems Fire, egress, and mechanical coordination happens inside one team
Schedule-sensitive financing Overlapping design and construction compresses the timeline
Single accountability One entity answers for the whole outcome, not two pointing fingers

The repetition point is the sharpest. In a single-family house a poorly-detailed connection is one problem; in a hundred-unit building it is a hundred problems, and the money saved by catching it during design rather than in the field multiplies accordingly. Design-build's continuous cost and constructability feedback is what catches it.

Speed Through Overlap

Because one entity controls both phases, design and construction can overlap rather than running strictly in sequence - foundations can begin while upper-floor details are finalized, a practice sometimes called fast-tracking. On a project carrying a construction loan whose interest accrues daily, compressing the schedule this way translates directly into saved carrying cost. The traditional model, with its clean handoff from a finished design to a bidding process, cannot overlap the phases the same way, which is one reason schedule-driven multi-family projects lean toward integrated delivery.

The Trade-Off: Oversight for Integration

Design-build's efficiency comes at the cost of the independent check that separated delivery provides. When the designer and builder are the same entity, the developer loses the architect who, in the traditional model, watches the builder's work on the owner's behalf. This is not a fatal flaw - it is a reason to vet a design-build firm carefully, review its completed portfolio, and structure the contract with transparency into costs and decisions. Developers who choose design-build trade an independent overseer for a single accountable partner, and the trade favors them when the partner is proven. Evaluating that partner and structuring the relationship is part of the broader builder-led delivery decision.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is design-build?

A delivery model where one entity holds both the design and construction of a project under a single contract, rather than the developer hiring a separate architect and builder. It creates one point of accountability and lets design and construction inform each other continuously.

How is design-build different from design-bid-build?

Design-bid-build is sequential and split: the design is finished, then bid to a separate builder under a second contract. Design-build combines both roles in one entity, so the internal handoffs happen within one organization rather than across contracts the developer must referee.

Why is design-build common for apartment buildings?

Because multi-family projects multiply every design decision across many units, run complex engineered systems, and carry schedule-sensitive financing. Design-build's early, continuous construction input catches costly issues before they are built dozens of times, and its overlapping phases compress the schedule.

What is fast-tracking?

Overlapping design and construction so that early work - like foundations - begins before later design details are complete. It is possible under design-build because one entity controls both phases, and it compresses the timeline, saving carrying cost on the construction loan.

Does design-build reduce design quality?

Not inherently - design quality depends on the designers involved, and established design-build firms employ or partner with licensed architects and engineers. The key is reviewing the firm's completed work, since the model removes the independent architect who would otherwise check the builder.

What does the developer give up with design-build?

The independent oversight of a separate architect watching the builder on the owner's behalf. The developer trades that check for a single accountable partner, which is why careful vetting and a transparent contract matter more under design-build.

Is design-build always the right choice?

No - it suits developers who value speed, single accountability, and integrated budgeting, but a developer with a trusted independent architect and a fully documented design may prefer the traditional path or a construction-manager arrangement. The best model depends on the project and the team.

How does design-build affect the construction loan?

Favorably, in most cases - lenders value the schedule certainty and single point of accountability an integrated team provides, and the compressed timeline from overlapping design and construction reduces the interest that accrues before the building earns income. A design-build entity delivering a firm price early also gives the lender a clearer basis for sizing the loan, which can smooth the financing process compared with waiting for competitive bids on a finished design.

Can a developer bring their own architect into a design-build project?

Often yes, through a hybrid arrangement where the developer's architect handles concept design and the design-build entity develops the construction documents and builds. This preserves some independent design voice while keeping the constructability and cost advantages of integrated delivery. The responsibility boundaries between the two need to be defined clearly in writing so the single-accountability benefit is not lost.

Developer, Builder, and GC Roles Explained

item.title: Developer, Builder, and GC Roles Explained
item.description:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

Who Does What in a Multi-Family Project

Confusion about who is responsible for what causes more friction on development projects than almost any technical problem. The roles have overlapping names - developer, builder, general contractor, developer-builder - and in different projects the same word can mean different things. Sorting them out is not academic: the role structure determines who carries which risk, who profits from what, and where the decisions actually get made. Understanding the distinct functions, and how they combine in different delivery models, is foundational to reading how any multi-family project is organized.

The Developer: Origin and Risk

The developer is the entity that makes the project happen. This is the party that identifies the opportunity, controls the land, secures the entitlements, arranges the financing, and carries the financial risk - and, if the project succeeds, captures the profit. The developer owns the vision and the outcome; everyone else is engaged to serve it. Critically, the developer's role is about capital and coordination, not swinging hammers: a developer can be a person, a partnership, or an institution, and many successful developers never physically build anything, instead assembling and directing the teams that do.

The General Contractor: Building the Building

The general contractor, or GC, is the party responsible for the physical construction. The GC holds the construction contract, manages the subcontractors and trades, schedules and sequences the work, controls the site, and delivers the finished structure. On a traditional project the GC is a separate firm the developer hires after the design is complete. The GC's expertise is execution - turning drawings into a building on schedule and on budget - and its profit comes from managing that construction efficiently, distinct from the developer's profit on the project as a whole.

Role Owns Profits from
Developer Land, financing, entitlements, the outcome The project's overall success
General contractor The construction contract and site Managing construction efficiently
Subcontractors Their specific trade scope Their trade work
Developer-builder Both the project and the construction Both - combined margins, aligned incentives

The Developer-Builder: One Party, Aligned Incentives

The developer-builder combines the two primary roles: the same firm that originates and owns the project also constructs it, holding both the developer's risk and the general contractor's execution responsibility. This alignment is the model's defining advantage. In separated delivery, the developer wants the lowest construction cost and the GC wants the highest construction profit - a built-in tension negotiated through the contract. When one firm is both, that tension disappears; every construction decision is made by the party that also owns the finished building and will live with its quality, its lease-up, and its reputation. The developer-builder profits from doing the whole thing well rather than from winning one side of an internal negotiation.

Why the Structure Matters to a Project

The role structure shapes behavior. Separated roles bring specialization and an arm's-length check but also friction at every interface, where each party optimizes its own piece. Combined roles bring alignment and speed but concentrate responsibility, so the combined firm's competence and integrity matter enormously. Neither is universally right - a first-time developer may need a strong independent GC's expertise, while an experienced developer-builder captures efficiencies no separated team can. What matters is understanding which structure a project uses, because it predicts where the decisions get made and whose interests drive them. That structural choice is a central part of the builder-led delivery decision that shapes a project from the start.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between a developer and a general contractor?

The developer originates and owns the project - controlling land, financing, and entitlements and carrying the risk - while the general contractor builds it, holding the construction contract and managing the trades. The developer profits from the project's success; the GC profits from managing construction efficiently.

What is a developer-builder?

A firm that combines both primary roles: it originates and owns the project and also constructs it, holding the developer's risk and the contractor's execution responsibility together. This aligns incentives, since the party building the project also owns and lives with the finished result.

Does the developer physically build anything?

Usually not directly - the developer's role is capital and coordination, assembling and directing the teams that design and build. Many successful developers never construct anything themselves, engaging general contractors and designers to execute their vision.

Why does combining roles align incentives?

In separated delivery the developer wants low construction cost and the GC wants high construction profit, a built-in tension. When one firm is both, that tension disappears - every construction decision is made by the party that also owns the finished building and its long-term performance.

What do subcontractors do?

Subcontractors perform specific trade scopes - framing, plumbing, electrical, and so on - under the general contractor, who coordinates and schedules them. They profit from their trade work, while the GC profits from managing the overall construction.

Is a developer-builder always better than separate parties?

No - combined roles bring alignment and speed but concentrate responsibility, so the firm's competence matters greatly. A first-time developer may benefit from a strong independent contractor's expertise, while an experienced developer-builder captures efficiencies separated teams cannot.

How does the role structure affect a project's decisions?

It determines where decisions get made and whose interests drive them. Separated roles add specialization and an arm's-length check but create friction at each handoff; combined roles add alignment and speed but rely on one firm's integrity. Knowing the structure predicts how the project will behave.

What is a construction manager, and how does that role fit in?

A construction manager advises the developer on cost, schedule, and constructability during design, then often builds the project under a guaranteed price - a role that sits between the fully separated general contractor and the combined developer-builder. It brings construction expertise in early, like an integrated model, while keeping the construction manager as a distinct party the developer engages. It is a common middle path for developers who want early builder input without merging the roles entirely.

Do developer-builders need both a developer's and a contractor's license?

Typically the construction side must hold the appropriate contractor licensing required in the jurisdiction, since that is regulated, while "developer" is a business role rather than a licensed profession. A developer-builder therefore operates under the construction licenses its building arm carries, plus whatever registrations local law requires, and confirming that licensing is in order is part of vetting any firm that claims to do both.

Construction Loans and Draw Schedules Explained
item.content:encoded:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

Who Does What in a Multi-Family Project

Confusion about who is responsible for what causes more friction on development projects than almost any technical problem. The roles have overlapping names - developer, builder, general contractor, developer-builder - and in different projects the same word can mean different things. Sorting them out is not academic: the role structure determines who carries which risk, who profits from what, and where the decisions actually get made. Understanding the distinct functions, and how they combine in different delivery models, is foundational to reading how any multi-family project is organized.

The Developer: Origin and Risk

The developer is the entity that makes the project happen. This is the party that identifies the opportunity, controls the land, secures the entitlements, arranges the financing, and carries the financial risk - and, if the project succeeds, captures the profit. The developer owns the vision and the outcome; everyone else is engaged to serve it. Critically, the developer's role is about capital and coordination, not swinging hammers: a developer can be a person, a partnership, or an institution, and many successful developers never physically build anything, instead assembling and directing the teams that do.

The General Contractor: Building the Building

The general contractor, or GC, is the party responsible for the physical construction. The GC holds the construction contract, manages the subcontractors and trades, schedules and sequences the work, controls the site, and delivers the finished structure. On a traditional project the GC is a separate firm the developer hires after the design is complete. The GC's expertise is execution - turning drawings into a building on schedule and on budget - and its profit comes from managing that construction efficiently, distinct from the developer's profit on the project as a whole.

Role Owns Profits from
Developer Land, financing, entitlements, the outcome The project's overall success
General contractor The construction contract and site Managing construction efficiently
Subcontractors Their specific trade scope Their trade work
Developer-builder Both the project and the construction Both - combined margins, aligned incentives

The Developer-Builder: One Party, Aligned Incentives

The developer-builder combines the two primary roles: the same firm that originates and owns the project also constructs it, holding both the developer's risk and the general contractor's execution responsibility. This alignment is the model's defining advantage. In separated delivery, the developer wants the lowest construction cost and the GC wants the highest construction profit - a built-in tension negotiated through the contract. When one firm is both, that tension disappears; every construction decision is made by the party that also owns the finished building and will live with its quality, its lease-up, and its reputation. The developer-builder profits from doing the whole thing well rather than from winning one side of an internal negotiation.

Why the Structure Matters to a Project

The role structure shapes behavior. Separated roles bring specialization and an arm's-length check but also friction at every interface, where each party optimizes its own piece. Combined roles bring alignment and speed but concentrate responsibility, so the combined firm's competence and integrity matter enormously. Neither is universally right - a first-time developer may need a strong independent GC's expertise, while an experienced developer-builder captures efficiencies no separated team can. What matters is understanding which structure a project uses, because it predicts where the decisions get made and whose interests drive them. That structural choice is a central part of the builder-led delivery decision that shapes a project from the start.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between a developer and a general contractor?

The developer originates and owns the project - controlling land, financing, and entitlements and carrying the risk - while the general contractor builds it, holding the construction contract and managing the trades. The developer profits from the project's success; the GC profits from managing construction efficiently.

What is a developer-builder?

A firm that combines both primary roles: it originates and owns the project and also constructs it, holding the developer's risk and the contractor's execution responsibility together. This aligns incentives, since the party building the project also owns and lives with the finished result.

Does the developer physically build anything?

Usually not directly - the developer's role is capital and coordination, assembling and directing the teams that design and build. Many successful developers never construct anything themselves, engaging general contractors and designers to execute their vision.

Why does combining roles align incentives?

In separated delivery the developer wants low construction cost and the GC wants high construction profit, a built-in tension. When one firm is both, that tension disappears - every construction decision is made by the party that also owns the finished building and its long-term performance.

What do subcontractors do?

Subcontractors perform specific trade scopes - framing, plumbing, electrical, and so on - under the general contractor, who coordinates and schedules them. They profit from their trade work, while the GC profits from managing the overall construction.

Is a developer-builder always better than separate parties?

No - combined roles bring alignment and speed but concentrate responsibility, so the firm's competence matters greatly. A first-time developer may benefit from a strong independent contractor's expertise, while an experienced developer-builder captures efficiencies separated teams cannot.

How does the role structure affect a project's decisions?

It determines where decisions get made and whose interests drive them. Separated roles add specialization and an arm's-length check but create friction at each handoff; combined roles add alignment and speed but rely on one firm's integrity. Knowing the structure predicts how the project will behave.

What is a construction manager, and how does that role fit in?

A construction manager advises the developer on cost, schedule, and constructability during design, then often builds the project under a guaranteed price - a role that sits between the fully separated general contractor and the combined developer-builder. It brings construction expertise in early, like an integrated model, while keeping the construction manager as a distinct party the developer engages. It is a common middle path for developers who want early builder input without merging the roles entirely.

Do developer-builders need both a developer's and a contractor's license?

Typically the construction side must hold the appropriate contractor licensing required in the jurisdiction, since that is regulated, while "developer" is a business role rather than a licensed profession. A developer-builder therefore operates under the construction licenses its building arm carries, plus whatever registrations local law requires, and confirming that licensing is in order is part of vetting any firm that claims to do both.

Construction Loans and Draw Schedules Explained

item.title: Construction Loans and Draw Schedules Explained
item.description:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

How a Building Gets Paid For While It Rises

A finished apartment building can be worth tens of millions, but on the day construction starts it is worth only the land and a set of drawings. Financing a project through that gap - funding months of construction before a single unit earns rent - requires a distinctive instrument: the construction loan. Unlike a mortgage on a finished building, a construction loan releases money gradually as the building takes shape, and the mechanics of that release govern the project's cash flow, schedule, and stress. Anyone involved in delivering multi-family work needs to understand how construction financing actually moves, because the money's rhythm sets the project's rhythm.

Why Construction Loans Work Differently

A lender will not hand a developer the full construction budget on day one, because on day one there is nothing built to secure it. Instead the loan is committed up front but disbursed in installments - called draws - released only as verified work is completed. The building itself is the collateral, and the lender advances money against value that physically exists at each stage. This protects the lender, who never has more money out than there is building to back it, and it disciplines the project, since each dollar must be justified by progress before it arrives.

The Draw Schedule

The draw schedule is the agreed plan for how and when the loan releases funds, tied to construction milestones or to a regular cycle of verified progress. A typical structure releases money as phases complete - foundation, framing, dry-in, mechanical rough-in, finishes - with each draw requiring documentation and often an inspection before the lender funds it.

Draw stage (typical)Released when
Sitework and foundationExcavation and foundations verified complete
Framing and structureStructure stands and is inspected
Dry-in and rough-insWeather-tight; mechanical/electrical/plumbing roughed
FinishesInterior finishes progress toward completion
Final / retainage releaseCompletion, certificate of occupancy, punch list

Each draw request is supported by documentation - a schedule of values showing what portion of each line item is complete, lien waivers from subcontractors confirming they have been paid, and often a third-party inspection verifying the work exists. The lender funds the draw only after this package satisfies it, which is why draw administration is a real job on a multi-family project, not a formality.

Retainage and the Cash-Flow Squeeze

Two features make construction financing a constant balancing act. First, retainage: the lender (and the developer, from subcontractors) typically withholds a percentage of each payment - often five to ten percent - until the project is complete, ensuring everyone stays motivated to finish. That withheld money is earned but not yet paid, so the builder carries it. Second, the timing gap: work must be performed and often paid for before the draw reimbursing it arrives, so the builder and subcontractors front costs and wait. A project that runs draws smoothly keeps everyone liquid; one that lets documentation lag or work outpace the schedule creates a cash-flow squeeze that can stall the job even when the loan itself is sound.

Why Financing Fluency Is a Delivery Skill

The draw rhythm is why construction financing belongs in any discussion of project delivery rather than being walled off in a finance department. A builder who understands what each draw requires - the documentation, the inspections, the lien waivers - assembles clean draw packages that fund on time and keep the project's cash moving. One who treats the loan as someone else's problem discovers the schedule stalling while everyone waits on a rejected draw. Financing fluency, in other words, is part of building well, and coordinating it with construction is a core competency of builder-led delivery.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a construction loan?

A loan that funds a project's construction in stages rather than all at once, releasing money as verified work is completed. The building itself is the collateral, and the lender advances funds against the value that physically exists at each stage.

What is a draw schedule?

The agreed plan for how and when a construction loan releases funds, tied to milestones or verified progress. Money is released as phases complete - foundation, framing, dry-in, finishes - with each draw requiring documentation and often inspection before funding.

What documentation does a draw require?

Typically a schedule of values showing completion of each line item, lien waivers from subcontractors confirming payment, and often a third-party inspection verifying the work exists. The lender funds the draw only after this package satisfies it.

What is retainage?

A percentage of each payment - commonly five to ten percent - withheld until the project is complete, keeping everyone motivated to finish. It is money earned but not yet paid, which the builder and subcontractors carry until the final release.

Why do builders have to front costs?

Because work is performed, and often paid for, before the draw reimbursing it arrives. This timing gap means builders and subcontractors advance costs and wait for the loan to catch up, which is why smooth draw administration matters so much to cash flow.

What happens if a draw is delayed or rejected?

The project can stall for lack of cash even when the loan is fundamentally sound, because the money to continue is stuck. Incomplete documentation, work outpacing the verified schedule, or failed inspections are common causes - which is why clean draw packages are essential.

Why is construction financing part of project delivery?

Because the schedule runs on the rhythm of the draws. A builder who understands each draw's requirements keeps funds flowing and the project liquid; treating the loan as a separate concern invites stalls. Coordinating financing with construction is a core delivery skill.

What happens to the construction loan when the building is finished?

It is typically replaced by permanent financing - a longer-term mortgage on the completed, income-producing building, in a step often called the takeout. The construction loan is short-term and higher-cost by design, so refinancing it into permanent debt once the building earns rent lowers the carrying cost and stabilizes the project financially. Reaching that takeout sooner is a major reason developers push to complete and lease up efficiently.

What is a schedule of values?

A document that breaks the total contract price into line items - foundation, framing, mechanical, finishes, and so on - each assigned a dollar value. At each draw, the builder marks what percentage of each line item is complete, and that becomes the basis for the amount requested. It gives the lender and the developer a clear, itemized view of progress against money, and keeping it accurate is central to clean draw administration.

Project Phasing and Certificate of Occupancy
item.content:encoded:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

How a Building Gets Paid For While It Rises

A finished apartment building can be worth tens of millions, but on the day construction starts it is worth only the land and a set of drawings. Financing a project through that gap - funding months of construction before a single unit earns rent - requires a distinctive instrument: the construction loan. Unlike a mortgage on a finished building, a construction loan releases money gradually as the building takes shape, and the mechanics of that release govern the project's cash flow, schedule, and stress. Anyone involved in delivering multi-family work needs to understand how construction financing actually moves, because the money's rhythm sets the project's rhythm.

Why Construction Loans Work Differently

A lender will not hand a developer the full construction budget on day one, because on day one there is nothing built to secure it. Instead the loan is committed up front but disbursed in installments - called draws - released only as verified work is completed. The building itself is the collateral, and the lender advances money against value that physically exists at each stage. This protects the lender, who never has more money out than there is building to back it, and it disciplines the project, since each dollar must be justified by progress before it arrives.

The Draw Schedule

The draw schedule is the agreed plan for how and when the loan releases funds, tied to construction milestones or to a regular cycle of verified progress. A typical structure releases money as phases complete - foundation, framing, dry-in, mechanical rough-in, finishes - with each draw requiring documentation and often an inspection before the lender funds it.

Draw stage (typical)Released when
Sitework and foundationExcavation and foundations verified complete
Framing and structureStructure stands and is inspected
Dry-in and rough-insWeather-tight; mechanical/electrical/plumbing roughed
FinishesInterior finishes progress toward completion
Final / retainage releaseCompletion, certificate of occupancy, punch list

Each draw request is supported by documentation - a schedule of values showing what portion of each line item is complete, lien waivers from subcontractors confirming they have been paid, and often a third-party inspection verifying the work exists. The lender funds the draw only after this package satisfies it, which is why draw administration is a real job on a multi-family project, not a formality.

Retainage and the Cash-Flow Squeeze

Two features make construction financing a constant balancing act. First, retainage: the lender (and the developer, from subcontractors) typically withholds a percentage of each payment - often five to ten percent - until the project is complete, ensuring everyone stays motivated to finish. That withheld money is earned but not yet paid, so the builder carries it. Second, the timing gap: work must be performed and often paid for before the draw reimbursing it arrives, so the builder and subcontractors front costs and wait. A project that runs draws smoothly keeps everyone liquid; one that lets documentation lag or work outpace the schedule creates a cash-flow squeeze that can stall the job even when the loan itself is sound.

Why Financing Fluency Is a Delivery Skill

The draw rhythm is why construction financing belongs in any discussion of project delivery rather than being walled off in a finance department. A builder who understands what each draw requires - the documentation, the inspections, the lien waivers - assembles clean draw packages that fund on time and keep the project's cash moving. One who treats the loan as someone else's problem discovers the schedule stalling while everyone waits on a rejected draw. Financing fluency, in other words, is part of building well, and coordinating it with construction is a core competency of builder-led delivery.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a construction loan?

A loan that funds a project's construction in stages rather than all at once, releasing money as verified work is completed. The building itself is the collateral, and the lender advances funds against the value that physically exists at each stage.

What is a draw schedule?

The agreed plan for how and when a construction loan releases funds, tied to milestones or verified progress. Money is released as phases complete - foundation, framing, dry-in, finishes - with each draw requiring documentation and often inspection before funding.

What documentation does a draw require?

Typically a schedule of values showing completion of each line item, lien waivers from subcontractors confirming payment, and often a third-party inspection verifying the work exists. The lender funds the draw only after this package satisfies it.

What is retainage?

A percentage of each payment - commonly five to ten percent - withheld until the project is complete, keeping everyone motivated to finish. It is money earned but not yet paid, which the builder and subcontractors carry until the final release.

Why do builders have to front costs?

Because work is performed, and often paid for, before the draw reimbursing it arrives. This timing gap means builders and subcontractors advance costs and wait for the loan to catch up, which is why smooth draw administration matters so much to cash flow.

What happens if a draw is delayed or rejected?

The project can stall for lack of cash even when the loan is fundamentally sound, because the money to continue is stuck. Incomplete documentation, work outpacing the verified schedule, or failed inspections are common causes - which is why clean draw packages are essential.

Why is construction financing part of project delivery?

Because the schedule runs on the rhythm of the draws. A builder who understands each draw's requirements keeps funds flowing and the project liquid; treating the loan as a separate concern invites stalls. Coordinating financing with construction is a core delivery skill.

What happens to the construction loan when the building is finished?

It is typically replaced by permanent financing - a longer-term mortgage on the completed, income-producing building, in a step often called the takeout. The construction loan is short-term and higher-cost by design, so refinancing it into permanent debt once the building earns rent lowers the carrying cost and stabilizes the project financially. Reaching that takeout sooner is a major reason developers push to complete and lease up efficiently.

What is a schedule of values?

A document that breaks the total contract price into line items - foundation, framing, mechanical, finishes, and so on - each assigned a dollar value. At each draw, the builder marks what percentage of each line item is complete, and that becomes the basis for the amount requested. It gives the lender and the developer a clear, itemized view of progress against money, and keeping it accurate is central to clean draw administration.

Project Phasing and Certificate of Occupancy

item.title: Project Phasing and Certificate of Occupancy
item.description:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

Finishing Construction Is Not Finishing the Project

There is a moment on every multi-family project when the building looks done - the exterior is complete, the units are finished - and yet no one can legally move in. Between the last coat of paint and the first tenant lies a formal gate: the certificate of occupancy, the government's declaration that the building is safe to inhabit. And on larger projects, the path to that gate is not a single finish line but a series of them, as the building opens in phases to start earning income before the whole project completes. Understanding occupancy and phasing is understanding the endgame of delivery, where a project finally turns from a cost into an asset.

The Certificate of Occupancy

The certificate of occupancy, or CO, is the official document a municipality issues confirming that a building complies with code and is safe for its intended use. Until it is issued, occupancy is not legal - a building can be physically finished and still empty because the paperwork gate has not opened. Earning the CO requires passing the final inspections across every discipline: building, fire, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and accessibility, each confirming its systems work and comply. The fire and life-safety inspections are especially consequential in multi-family, since the sprinklers, alarms, rated assemblies, and egress must all be verified operational before people live among them.

Temporary and Phased Occupancy

Large multi-family projects rarely finish all at once, and waiting for the entire development to complete before earning any income would be financially punishing. So the building often opens in stages, each stage cleared for occupancy as it finishes:

MechanismWhat it allows
Temporary certificate of occupancy (TCO)Move-in before the whole project is finished, with conditions
Phased occupancy by buildingCompleted buildings open while others are under construction
Phased occupancy by floorLower floors occupied while upper floors finish
Final certificate of occupancyFull, unconditional occupancy at project completion

A temporary certificate of occupancy permits move-in before every last item is done, provided the occupied portion is safe and specific conditions are met - the life-safety systems serving those units must be fully operational, even if landscaping or a later building is not. Phasing lets a completed building or a block of lower floors begin leasing and generating rent while construction continues elsewhere on site, which transforms the project's finances.

Why Phasing Is a Financial Strategy

Phased occupancy is not merely a convenience; it is a deliberate financial tool. Every month a finished unit sits empty waiting for the rest of the project is a month of lost rent against a loan still accruing interest. By opening in phases, a developer starts lease-up early, brings in income that offsets carrying costs, and begins the transition from a construction loan to permanent financing sooner. The sequencing has to be planned from the start - the site logistics, the utility connections, and the life-safety systems must all be arranged so that early phases can be safely occupied while later phases are active construction zones, with the two kept separated. This is delivery and finance intertwined, and getting it right materially improves a project's return.

The Endgame Belongs in the Plan From the Beginning

Because occupancy and phasing carry such financial weight, they cannot be improvised at the end. The construction sequence, the draw schedule, the inspection timeline, and the lease-up plan all have to align so that the building reaches its occupancy gates when the financing and the market need it to. A project planned backward from its occupancy strategy - knowing which phase must open when, and building toward those dates - outperforms one that treats the CO as an afterthought discovered at the finish. Sequencing that endgame alongside the construction is one of the defining skills of builder-led delivery, where the party building the project also understands what it takes to open and lease it.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a certificate of occupancy?

The official document a municipality issues confirming a building complies with code and is safe for its intended use. Until it is issued, legal occupancy is not permitted - a building can be physically finished yet remain empty until the CO is granted.

What is required to earn a certificate of occupancy?

Passing final inspections across every discipline - building, fire, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and accessibility. In multi-family, the fire and life-safety inspections are especially critical, since sprinklers, alarms, rated assemblies, and egress must all be verified operational.

What is a temporary certificate of occupancy?

A TCO permits move-in before the entire project is finished, provided the occupied portion is safe and specific conditions are met. The life-safety systems serving those units must be fully operational even if outstanding items like landscaping or a later building remain.

What is phased occupancy?

Opening a large project in stages - building by building or floor by floor - so completed portions can be occupied and start earning rent while construction continues elsewhere. It lets a project generate income before the whole development is finished.

Why is phasing a financial strategy?

Because empty finished units lose rent while the loan still accrues interest. Phasing starts lease-up early, brings in income that offsets carrying costs, and speeds the shift from construction loan to permanent financing - materially improving the project's return.

Can people move in before the whole project is done?

Yes, under a temporary certificate of occupancy or phased occupancy, as long as the occupied portion is safe and its life-safety systems are operational. Early phases are occupied while later phases remain active construction, with the two kept separated.

Why must the occupancy plan be set early?

Because the construction sequence, draw schedule, inspections, and lease-up must all align to hit the occupancy gates when financing and the market require. A project planned backward from its occupancy strategy outperforms one that treats the certificate of occupancy as an afterthought.

What is the difference between substantial completion and a certificate of occupancy?

Substantial completion is a construction-contract milestone meaning the building is finished enough for its intended use, with only minor punch-list items remaining - it triggers contract events like final payment timing. The certificate of occupancy is the government's separate legal permission to inhabit the building, earned by passing inspections. A project can be substantially complete yet still awaiting its CO, so the two milestones are related but distinct.

What is a punch list?

The list of small, outstanding items - a scratched surface, a missing fixture, a touch-up - identified near the end of construction that must be corrected to fully complete the work. Punch-list items are typically minor enough that they do not prevent occupancy, and completing them is often tied to the release of final payment and retainage. Managing the punch list efficiently is part of closing out a project cleanly.

Builder-Led Delivery of Multi-Family: A Primer
item.content:encoded:
By the Editorial Team | Published: July 2026 | Last Updated: July 2026

Finishing Construction Is Not Finishing the Project

There is a moment on every multi-family project when the building looks done - the exterior is complete, the units are finished - and yet no one can legally move in. Between the last coat of paint and the first tenant lies a formal gate: the certificate of occupancy, the government's declaration that the building is safe to inhabit. And on larger projects, the path to that gate is not a single finish line but a series of them, as the building opens in phases to start earning income before the whole project completes. Understanding occupancy and phasing is understanding the endgame of delivery, where a project finally turns from a cost into an asset.

The Certificate of Occupancy

The certificate of occupancy, or CO, is the official document a municipality issues confirming that a building complies with code and is safe for its intended use. Until it is issued, occupancy is not legal - a building can be physically finished and still empty because the paperwork gate has not opened. Earning the CO requires passing the final inspections across every discipline: building, fire, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and accessibility, each confirming its systems work and comply. The fire and life-safety inspections are especially consequential in multi-family, since the sprinklers, alarms, rated assemblies, and egress must all be verified operational before people live among them.

Temporary and Phased Occupancy

Large multi-family projects rarely finish all at once, and waiting for the entire development to complete before earning any income would be financially punishing. So the building often opens in stages, each stage cleared for occupancy as it finishes:

MechanismWhat it allows
Temporary certificate of occupancy (TCO)Move-in before the whole project is finished, with conditions
Phased occupancy by buildingCompleted buildings open while others are under construction
Phased occupancy by floorLower floors occupied while upper floors finish
Final certificate of occupancyFull, unconditional occupancy at project completion

A temporary certificate of occupancy permits move-in before every last item is done, provided the occupied portion is safe and specific conditions are met - the life-safety systems serving those units must be fully operational, even if landscaping or a later building is not. Phasing lets a completed building or a block of lower floors begin leasing and generating rent while construction continues elsewhere on site, which transforms the project's finances.

Why Phasing Is a Financial Strategy

Phased occupancy is not merely a convenience; it is a deliberate financial tool. Every month a finished unit sits empty waiting for the rest of the project is a month of lost rent against a loan still accruing interest. By opening in phases, a developer starts lease-up early, brings in income that offsets carrying costs, and begins the transition from a construction loan to permanent financing sooner. The sequencing has to be planned from the start - the site logistics, the utility connections, and the life-safety systems must all be arranged so that early phases can be safely occupied while later phases are active construction zones, with the two kept separated. This is delivery and finance intertwined, and getting it right materially improves a project's return.

The Endgame Belongs in the Plan From the Beginning

Because occupancy and phasing carry such financial weight, they cannot be improvised at the end. The construction sequence, the draw schedule, the inspection timeline, and the lease-up plan all have to align so that the building reaches its occupancy gates when the financing and the market need it to. A project planned backward from its occupancy strategy - knowing which phase must open when, and building toward those dates - outperforms one that treats the CO as an afterthought discovered at the finish. Sequencing that endgame alongside the construction is one of the defining skills of builder-led delivery, where the party building the project also understands what it takes to open and lease it.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a certificate of occupancy?

The official document a municipality issues confirming a building complies with code and is safe for its intended use. Until it is issued, legal occupancy is not permitted - a building can be physically finished yet remain empty until the CO is granted.

What is required to earn a certificate of occupancy?

Passing final inspections across every discipline - building, fire, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and accessibility. In multi-family, the fire and life-safety inspections are especially critical, since sprinklers, alarms, rated assemblies, and egress must all be verified operational.

What is a temporary certificate of occupancy?

A TCO permits move-in before the entire project is finished, provided the occupied portion is safe and specific conditions are met. The life-safety systems serving those units must be fully operational even if outstanding items like landscaping or a later building remain.

What is phased occupancy?

Opening a large project in stages - building by building or floor by floor - so completed portions can be occupied and start earning rent while construction continues elsewhere. It lets a project generate income before the whole development is finished.

Why is phasing a financial strategy?

Because empty finished units lose rent while the loan still accrues interest. Phasing starts lease-up early, brings in income that offsets carrying costs, and speeds the shift from construction loan to permanent financing - materially improving the project's return.

Can people move in before the whole project is done?

Yes, under a temporary certificate of occupancy or phased occupancy, as long as the occupied portion is safe and its life-safety systems are operational. Early phases are occupied while later phases remain active construction, with the two kept separated.

Why must the occupancy plan be set early?

Because the construction sequence, draw schedule, inspections, and lease-up must all align to hit the occupancy gates when financing and the market require. A project planned backward from its occupancy strategy outperforms one that treats the certificate of occupancy as an afterthought.

What is the difference between substantial completion and a certificate of occupancy?

Substantial completion is a construction-contract milestone meaning the building is finished enough for its intended use, with only minor punch-list items remaining - it triggers contract events like final payment timing. The certificate of occupancy is the government's separate legal permission to inhabit the building, earned by passing inspections. A project can be substantially complete yet still awaiting its CO, so the two milestones are related but distinct.

What is a punch list?

The list of small, outstanding items - a scratched surface, a missing fixture, a touch-up - identified near the end of construction that must be corrected to fully complete the work. Punch-list items are typically minor enough that they do not prevent occupancy, and completing them is often tied to the release of final payment and retainage. Managing the punch list efficiently is part of closing out a project cleanly.

Builder-Led Delivery of Multi-Family: A Primer
